summary: Joe Reis argues against traditional New Year's resolutions in favor of building consistent daily habits and systems. He suggests that long-term success comes from compound improvements over years rather than cramming goals into a single year.
key_points: Most people fail goals due to having too many or lacking systems/habits for consistency
Daily habits and systems are more effective than annual resolutions
Success requires years of consistent improvement, not quick wins
Compound interest applies to personal development - small daily gains accumulate significantly
money_quotes: Systems, habits, and 'grinding it out' every day are how you get wherever you want.
Few things in life come easy, and everything takes longer than you think.
Success is often the result of many years of simply improving yourself and avoiding too many stupid things.
Several years from now, you'll look back with awe at how much skill, knowledge, and wisdom you've accumulated by simply doing the same boring thing over and over.
key_insight: Long-term success comes from boring, consistent daily habits rather than ambitious annual resolutions - let compound interest work in your favor.
